data.title: Li Auto: Highly Profitable EV Play
data.description:
Li Auto (NASDAQ:LI) missed estimates on the bottom and top line for its second-quarter on Thursday, despite the electric vehicle company maintaining high vehicle margins in its core manufacturing business. Li Auto, however, is seeing moderating top line growth and submitted a weak delivery forecast for the third fiscal quarter which disappointed investors. On the other hand, robust profitability limits downside risks with this specific EV stock, and since Li Auto still reports some of the highest vehicle margins in the industry, I believe the investment setup is still overall bullish for investors.

Previous rating

I recommended Li Auto as a strong buy in my last article -- A Top Growth Pick For 2025 -- because the electric vehicle company, together with BYD (OTCPK:BYDDF), was (and still is) leading the EV market in terms of gross margins. Li Auto also remained widely profitable in the second fiscal quarter, but the EV firm unfortunately guided for a weak delivery volume for Q3'25 which may create some negative sentiment overhang in the short term. I believe the company's high margins and general profitability outweigh any risks associated with moderating top line and delivery growth, and I continue to recommend the EV company to growth investors as a strong buy.

Strong margin profile and profitability in the core manufacturing business

Li Auto missed on both the bottom and top line in the second-quarter, although the EV enterprise remained profitable: the electric vehicle company generated $0.19 per-share in normalized earnings in Q2, missing the consensus estimate by $0.05 per-share. Second-quarter revenue totaled $4.24B which missed the average estimate by $206M.

In the second-quarter, the electric vehicle maker generated a total top line of 30.2B Chinese Yuan ($4.2B), showing a decline rate of 4.5% year-over-year. The EV company delivered 111,074 electric vehicles in Q2'25, showing only 2.3% growth compared to the year-earlier period.

Li Auto's slowing growth is due in part due to the company seeing increasing competition in the Chinese EV market, weaker demand for its L-Series EREV crossovers (EREV stands for Extended-Range EV) and a shift in consumer preferences towards lower-cost EV brands. However, Li Auto is launching new products which could help the electric vehicle maker improve its delivery trajectory going forward.

In July, the EV company debuted the Li i8, a six-seat all-electric SUV, in a bid to expand its BEV product portfolio. Li Auto began Li i8 deliveries on August 20, 2025 and the firm's management has laid out an ambitious target of ramping up deliveries to between 8,000 and 10,000 units by the end of September.

In July, Li Auto delivered 30,731 vehicles, showing a year-over-year decline of 40% and it was the second consecutive monthly delivery decline on a Y/Y basis for the EV company. Li Auto’s weak delivery performance lately is in part due to a weak market reception for the company’s L-Series -- which includes the L6, L7, L8 and L9 premium SUVs. Sales of these vehicles contracted between 40-53% year-over-year in July, showing a challenging situation for the EV company as far as the delivery trajectory is concerned.

On the other hand, the firm’s Mega EV, a battery-powered luxury minivan and multi-purpose vehicle, saw its deliveries surge to 2,816 units in July, showing a year-over-year growth rate of 330%.

While the delivery situation is challenged, the profitability picture looks very solid. Li Auto generated vehicle margins of 19.4% in Q2'25, showing a quarter-over-quarter decline of only 0.4 PP. For comparison, XPeng (XPEV), which already reported second-quarter earnings more than a week ago, disclosed vehicle margins of 14.3%. XPeng generated 3.8 PP margin growth in the second-quarter, on a sequential basis, so while the EV start-up did better than Li Auto in terms of growth, Li Auto still has by far the highest vehicle margins. NIO (NIO), which has not yet reported second-quarter results, had a vehicle margin of 10.2% in the first-quarter.

Overall, Li Auto maintained high levels of profitability as well, despite delivery headwinds. In Q2'25, the EV maker generated 1.5B Chinese Yuan ($204.9M) in non-GAAP earnings, showing 45% year-over-year growth.

Li Auto’s weak Q3 guidance

In terms of deliveries, Li Auto guided for a delivery volume of 90-95k units for the third-quarter, implying a drop-off rate of up to 41.1% year-over-year. The outlook was a disappointment and indicates that competitive pressures in the Chinese EV market are increasing. In the short term, the poor guidance for Q3'25 could create new negative sentiment overhang for shares of Li Auto... which I believe investors should see as an engagement opportunity.

Li Auto’s valuation

As I have argued many times in my previous coverage on the automaker, I see the company as top value in the Chinese electric vehicle start-up group. This is mainly due to Li Auto leading its rivals in terms of vehicle margins, due to its focus on higher-priced premium EVs. While the firm's top line and delivery growth is slowing, the EV company is widely profitable and seeing significant earnings expansion.

Currently, shares of Li Auto are priced at a forward (FY 2026) P/S ratio of 0.82X which makes the company cheaper than both XPeng and NIO. NIO lags its EV rivals in terms of vehicle margins (as discussed above), but the company is seeing strong momentum for its Onvo-branded EVs and has potential to double its valuation given its low price-to-revenue ratio of 0.9X.

I recently up-graded shares of XPeng to buy as well as the company is benefiting from both strong delivery growth as well as a margin expansion in its core business: Serious Margin Gains. If Li Auto were to revalue to its historical P/S ratio of 1.25X, then shares of the EV company would have a fair value in the neighborhood of $35.70/share... which is approximately 54% above the firm's current trading price. In order for shares to revalue higher, however, Li Auto would likely have to return to delivery growth (which the Li i8 could potentially deliver).

Risks with Li Auto

The biggest risk for Li Auto is a potentially disappointing delivery trajectory for the Li i8 SUV which is just starting to ramp up deliveries to a monthly run-rate of up to 10k units by the end of September. Another potential risk for the EV maker is that demand for electric vehicles has been shifting towards lower-cost EVs which could weigh on margins going forward and undermine the firm's position in the high-margin premium segment. However, because of its solid profitability, I believe that Li Auto is in the best position to absorb margin pressure. What would therefore change my mind about the EV maker is if Li Auto were to see a deterioration of its profitability profile or if Li i8 delivery projections didn't materialize.

Final thoughts

Li Auto submitted a decent earnings scorecard for the second fiscal quarter although the company missed on both earnings and revenue. While Li Auto faces some growth challenges and the Q3'25 delivery outlook was not especially great, the EV enterprise has a major advantage compared to many other EV plays, including those based in the U.S.: Li Auto is already widely profitable and is generating some of the highest vehicle margins in the industry which puts the company in a strong competitive position if margin pressures escalate. Further, Li Auto is also the cheapest electric vehicle play in the industry group, which is what I believe drastically limits investment risks for EV investors.

data.title: We Are Buying ECC Perpetual Preferred Stock Again
data.description:
Co-authored by Strategic Yield.

CLO stocks are among the favorite asset classes and popular among retail investors. Eagle Point Credit Co LLC (NYSE:ECC) continues to attract attention, despite high fees and the relatively low risk-adjusted return offered by its common stock. We're not fans of ECC's common stock, but we find its perpetual preferred stock very attractive. Today, we'll show you why we're once again betting on the perpetual preferred stock (NYSE:ECC.PR.D), which offers a yield of over 8%.

Company Overview

Founded in 2014 and headquartered in Greenwich, Connecticut, USA, Eagle Point Credit Company is a company that invests primarily in equity and junior debt tranches of collateralized loan obligations, or CLOs. ECC has approximately $1.52 billion in total assets, spread across 306 different investments. The company’s current market capitalization is approximately $928 million, with a leverage level of around 37% and an expense ratio of 10.51%, of which 2.15% is interest expenses and 2.06% is the management fees. The annualized distribution rate on NAV is close to 23%. Below is shown the ECC's portfolio of investments:

The credit quality of ECC is around a B score:

ECC’s common stock is trading at approximately $7.34 at the time of writing. However, its long-term yield has been disappointing compared to the fixed-income instruments that the company offers. Therefore, our focus today is on the perpetual preferred stock ECC.PR.D.

Our Pick

ECC.PR.D is currently trading at around $19.53, below par, and offers a current yield of 8.65%. It pays an annual cumulative dividend of 6.75%, which equates to $0.140625 per month or $1.6875 per year. ECC issued 1 million shares of ECC.PR.D with a par value of $25 each on 11/17/2021. The stock has a call date of 11/29/2026, with 1.25 years to call, and was rated BBB by Egan-Jones Ratings Co. at the time of its initial offering, although it is not rated by Moody’s, S&P, or Fitch. Below are the basic metrics of the stock:

Why do we choose ECC.PR.D?

  • -With an 8.65% CY and price below par, ECC.PR.D outperforms the historical real NAV return of ECC's common stock. ECC common stock often attracts attention for its high dividends, but they come with high fees and a lower return relative to risk.
  • -As a preferred stock, ECC.PR.D has priority over common stock in dividend distributions and in the event of liquidation.
  • - The company's leverage restrictions provide additional security for preferred stock investors.
  • - If we look at this chart based on current yields, with ECC.PR.D and its peers (fund stocks), we can see ECC.PR.D outperforms with the highest current yield:

ECC-D is supposed to have the highest current yield, and there is no doubt that having CLO equity as an asset is way riskier than having exchange-traded common stocks, as many of the other CEFs. But given the safety of the CEF structure and the regulatory leverage restrictions, we are ready to take on extra risk to boost our returns in a diversified portfolio.

Conclusion

Although Eagle Point Credit Company's common stock attracts investors with its high dividends, it comes with high fees and a lower risk-adjusted return. Instead, we recommend the perpetual preferred stock ECC.PR.D, which offers an attractive current yield of 8.65% and is trading below par. With priority in dividend distributions and liquidation, as well as the added security of the company's leverage restrictions, ECC.PR.D is a better alternative for investors looking for high and stable income. Compared to other similar stocks, ECC.PR.D has the highest current yield, making it attractive for investors willing to take extra risk and especially for those willing to take the risk of the common stock.

data.title: GoDaddy: Bookings And Renewal Pressure Underscore An Expensive Valuation (Downgrade)
data.description:
As we exit the bulk of the Q2 earnings season, the stock market is retreating nervously after closing near all-time highs. In my view, this is a clear signal that investors need to be focused on individual stock-picking. The stocks in our portfolios have to meet a high dual bar: high quality, and a reasonable valuation that leaves room for upside.

GoDaddy (NYSE:GDDY), the web hosting platform, is neither of these things. The company has been touting the higher attach rates on its AI product, GoDaddy Airo: but at the same time, the company hasn't been able to lift growth from its current high single digit pace, indicating that AI isn't really an accelerant for the company. And yet despite this, GoDaddy still trades at a premium to peers. Investors, meanwhile, are beginning to catch on to GoDaddy's relative lack of substance, with the stock declining by ~25% since the start of the year, with losses picking up steam after the company's recent Q2 earnings release.

I last wrote a neutral opinion on GoDaddy in June, when the stock was trading higher at $175 per share. Even though GoDaddy has declined nearly 20% since then, I'm not tempted at all to buy the dip in this name. I'm discouraged by GoDaddy's deceleration in its bookings growth rate, though I will acknowledge that the company has done a good job at keeping expense growth in check and boosting its adjusted EBITDA profile. I'm downgrading GoDaddy to a sell rating.

To me, there are a number of red flags and risk factors that investors need to be cognizant of here:

  • GoDaddy is seeing slowing bookings. It's difficult to claim that AI is an accelerant and a game-changer for your company, when the same company is also seeing bookings growth decelerate to the single digits. In my view, GoDaddy is simply treading water and claiming AI-powered features, while in reality it's not driving any meaningful new business from these releases.
  • Web hosting is a commoditized industry with little differentiation and plenty of competition. Web hosting sites may vary slightly on UI and features, but they basically all serve the same commoditized service. GoDaddy is among the slowest-growing of the group, compared to competitors like Wix and Shopify (SHOP).
  • Heavy SMB exposure amid a choppy macroeconomy: Since a good chunk of GoDaddy's business lies in entrepreneurs and small businesses, the tough macroeconomy and higher possibility of business failures may impact GoDaddy's retention rates.
  • Churn issues: GoDaddy is seeing its customer base shrink on a y/y basis, reflecting the company's difficulties with retaining paid subscribers: which could be a reflection of sharp competition, the macro, or both.
  • Net debt position: Unlike most tech companies, GoDaddy has a substantial debt load that far outweighs its cash, which limits its financial flexibility.

Despite its many risks, I find GoDaddy to still trade at an unbearable premium. At current share prices near $148, GoDaddy trades at a market cap of $20.43 billion. After we net off the $1.09 billion of cash against $3.79 billion of debt on GoDaddy's most recent balance sheet, the company's resulting enterprise value is $23.13 billion.

For the remainder of FY25, GoDaddy has guided to $4.89-$4.94 billion in revenue, or 7-8% growth (slightly higher at the low end relative to a prior wider outlook of $4.86-$4.94 billion, or 6-8% growth). It has also indicated the same +100bps of NEBITDA ("normalized" EBITDA, equivalent to other companies' adjusted EBITDA) expansion, or a 31% margin that now points to $1.52 billion in nominal NEBITDA.

For next year FY26, Wall Street analysts are expecting GoDaddy to generate $5.28 billion in revenue (a continuation of 7% growth). If we assume a further 100bps margin expansion to 32%, normalized EBITDA next year would be $1.69 billion (+11% y/y). This puts GoDaddy's valuation multiples at:

  • 4.7x EV/FY25 revenue and 4.4x EV/FY26 revenue
  • 15.2x EV/FY25 NEBITDA and 13.7x EV/FY26 NEBITDA

My continued concern with GoDaddy is that it's unfavorably positioned against its closest pure-play rival, Wix. GoDaddy trades at a slight premium in revenue terms against Wix. On an adjusted EBITDA basis, GoDaddy's ~15x multiple is ~10% lower than Wix - but Wix is also growing revenue in the low teens, at nearly double the pace of GoDaddy - so arguably Wix deserves that healthy premium.

All in all, I'm not convinced to pay a mid-teens EBITDA multiple for a company that is struggling to maintain high single digit revenue growth in a crowded, commoditized industry. I'd prefer to wait for GoDaddy's valuation to keep correcting downward (while rival Wix has been appreciating higher) before stepping in.

Q2 download

Let's now go through GoDaddy's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q2 earnings summary is shown below:

GoDaddy's revenue grew 8.3% y/y to $1.22 billion, slightly ahead of Wall Street's expectations of $1.21 billion (+7.3% y/y) by a one-point margin. Revenue kept pace with Q1's 7.7% growth rate: but where we are slightly concerned is the fact that GoDaddy's bookings of $1.35 billion grew only 6.6% y/y, underperforming revenue growth and decelerating 130bps versus 7.9% growth in Q1.

Ultimately, it is bookings that represents the longer-term arc of GoDaddy's growth trajectory: and the deceleration in bookings growth is a negative signal for the company's sales momentum. GoDaddy views one of its core functions as "bringing AI to micro businesses" - but despite the company's insistence that Airo adoption is increasing, the company isn't really seeing any benefits in monetization.

Management notes as well that macro conditions will put pressure on a spate of larger-client renewals later this year. Per CFO Mark McCaffrey's remarks on the Q2 earnings call:

On total customers, we have largely moved beyond the impacts of eliminating deep discounts and divestitures. That said, we continue to see some residual pressure from migrations as they move through their initial renewal cycles. Excluding this remaining headwind, customer count has grown in each of the last 2 months. As these pressures subside and we benefit from strong conversion of higher intent cohorts, we expect a return to customer growth later this year."

To that note, we highlight the fact that GoDaddy's count of 20.4 million customers declined by -2% y/y, though the company insists it can return to growth by the end of the year. Another headwind to note: starting later this year, GoDaddy will no longer operate as a registry service provider for ".co" domains, which the company says will negatively impact revenue and bookings growth by -50bps starting in the fourth quarter.

Where GoDaddy is offering a bit of compensation is on stronger margins. The company's normalized EBITDA grew 15% y/y to $381.7 million:

EBITDA margins improved 180bps y/y to 31.3%, driven by economies of scale on GoDaddy's raised pricing for its core subscriptions, and well ahead of the company's target of expanding margins at a 100bps y/y clip in FY25.

Key takeaways

To me, GoDaddy's ~15x forward adjusted EBITDA multiple is a tall ask for a company that is generating only single-digit revenue and bookings growth (of which a portion is being driven by pricing increases, and where customer counts are actually declining). In my view, we have to be very selective in stock-picking in today's market, and GoDaddy is both expensive and of questionable quality: in other words, it doesn't make the cut for my portfolio.

data.title: Mastercard: Moat Is Under Fire With Potentially Easing Or Tighter Regulation
data.description:
Mastercard (NYSE:MA) is one of the world's leading payment networks, as it fully benefits as the world transitions from cash to card, as cards would have a payment network such as Mastercard. They don't issue cards directly, but provide the infrastructure that connects the consumer to the merchant (or any other institution). Every time a Mastercard-branded card is used in a transaction, Mastercard earns a fee for routing and authorizing the transaction. The duo of Mastercard and Visa is indispensable, as card payments all require the network that they provide. However, with an already high valuation and adoption due to its moat and potentially high regulation, Mastercard's future could be quite foggy especially in a pro-crypto/tokenization society. Hence, I'm rating Mastercard at a 'Hold'.

Thesis

As the world continues to have a fundamental switch in payments from cash to cards, Mastercard continues to grow at a good pace. With Mastercard's moat, it is able to continue to dominate the network scene alongside Visa. Mastercard is a compound machine, as alongside double-digit growth and a small dividend rate, it is a top choice as a defensive stock in one's portfolio. Growth continues to accelerate, as the tariff headwinds in Q2 earnings have shown that the effects have been minimal, as guidance from the start of the year has not been changed or affected.

However, as the world's adoption of card payments is getting more and more saturated, further growth acceleration for Mastercard and upside may seem limited despite its moat. With regulations and many antitrust lawsuits, Mastercard is under pressure as increased regulations could negatively affect margins.

Furthermore, with the potential adoption of crypto payments and tokenization, the next shift in society could be towards using digital currency as payment, which avoids Mastercard's networks. With a high valuation already, any signs of its moat eroding or higher regulations would suggest the valuation is not justified. As President Trump favors Crypto, even launching his own coin, further deregulation of digital currency payments during his tenure of presidency could affect Mastercard's revenue in the US, which could lead to worldwide adoption if it is successful and scales up.

Mastercard's Moat

Society has shifted from cash payments to card payments. In the UK, cash was used for 51% of payments in 2013 with the number falling to 12% in just 2023. In the US, cash was used in 11% of in-store transactions. 82% of US adults hold at least 1 credit card, as card payments are simply the norm now in society.

Mastercard has a wide moat, as a huge reason is with Mastercard's and Visa's dominance in the whole network itself, as they account for 90% of all payment processing (excluding China). Mastercard connects the cardholder (consumer), through the cardholder's issuer (bank) that provides the cards, to the merchant's bank (merchant). Once a card is used on a card machine, it gets processed through the Mastercard network, as it routes to the issuer's bank for approval. Issuer checks for whether there are sufficient funds, sends a response back to the Mastercard network, which then ensures the funds are settled between both banks (consumer and merchant). Mastercard alongside Visa is indispensable in today's society, with many major cities becoming more and more cashless such as Central London where tourists flood the city.

Mastercard has a huge network effect. Acting as a neutral entity to settle and guarantee payments daily, it reduces the risk of fraud or each issuing bank trusting the other individually. They trust the payment processors like Visa and Mastercard instead. Mastercard operates in 210 countries with tens of millions of merchants using Mastercard's services. The number was around 37 million in 2023. Mastercard's strong network effect lies in its system being available worldwide. A consumer who has a card through Mastercard can only use the card if they accept the Mastercard processing system. As more and more countries adopt the use of Mastercard, the issuer's card can be used in more and more countries worldwide. This leads to users having an easier experience, as they wouldn't require cash payments in a foreign country as Mastercard as a processing system is accepted. If Mastercard is expanded to more and more countries, all users whose system runs through Mastercard will have a better experience if they travel to these countries as their card would work. For example, American Express (AXP) is not taken as often as Mastercard in the UK, due to having higher fees. This gives AXP users more limitations on where to use their card, since it is not as globally accepted as Mastercard in my view.

Mastercard's Regulation Scrutiny Eroding Its Moat

Mastercard also has a strong moat with its intangible assets and switching costs. This is one of the biggest dual-edged swords for Mastercard and Visa. It has huge brand trust and awareness, whilst switching costs to a competitor like AXP or operating in cash would be more expensive or miss out on potential revenue. However, is there any other choice for merchants to choose other than Mastercard and Visa? They dominate the market, as major cities taking Mastercard and Visa is now the norm. Since operating payment processing requires a huge network, such as operating in many countries or as many locations as possible, which also requires trust from the issuers initially, is simply impossible for competition to come in as they run the whole network (AXP can compete with Visa and Mastercard as they require the bank's trust, as they distribute the cards themselves whilst running the systems).

Since there is no other system to switch to, Visa and Mastercard tend to come under scrutiny as they are highly regulated, so fees are controlled. This protects the consumer, as with higher fees, merchants tend to pass the cost down to the consumer to protect margins, which increases the price of today's cost of living.

Mastercard faces litigation globally, coming from the UK, EU, Australia, and the US. These tend to be surrounding their interchange fees. A lot more details can be found within their 10-Q, but the idea is this could potentially cost Mastercard billions. There have been claims as high as £14B for a single claim, though only settled for £200M, the risk and fear will linger. If the US follows with lowering interchange fees like the EU with so much litigation pressure, it could indirectly affect Mastercard.

The EU (UK retained this law after Brexit) as a general rule capped the interchange fee of a transaction to 0.2% for debit card and 0.3% for credit card transactions (UK cross-border to EU countries payments are higher and are also under investigation). The USA is significantly different, with interchange fees as high as ~3%. How does this actually affect Mastercard if interchange fees are regulated?

A lot of the negative effects are directly on the card issuers themselves, as Mastercard gets the indirect effects. After the EU regulation, EU issuers are estimated to lose between €5–6 billion in interchange revenues. In the US, the high interchange fee leads to credit cards with rewards, as reward levels were decreased in the EU. This leads to an incentive for consumers to spend, as a key issue that is brought up in Congress is that Visa and Mastercard's monopolistic behaviors (higher interchange fees and fees for merchants) lead to an increasing credit card balance, as the debt per household continues to increase.

A lot of the credit card balance is influenced by high interest rates which are not regulated (not related to Mastercard), but the high rates set on the merchants force them to increase prices, which doesn't protect the customer, as it was noted in Congress that small businesses may have no choice but to pay the fee as Visa and Mastercard dominate the market. This doesn't put a good reputation on Mastercard in my opinion, as I think it comes to a point where either growth slows down for Mastercard since the gross dollar volume will fall since credit card debt is too high and individuals struggle to pay it off, or interchange fees getting capped, which will affect the gross dollar volume as consumers will spend less (assuming prices fall and consumers spend the same).

A lot of the idea doesn't affect Mastercard directly, but with prices in the US increasing with tariff pressure, as well as downward revisions on non-farm payrolls, the level of credit card balance for the average household gets to a point before it breaks. To avoid an even higher balance, consumers spend less, which affects Mastercard. The average balance is $6580, 46% of households have a carried balance, with revolving credit card debt hitting a record of $1.18 trillion, which is worrying. Mastercard's credit cards are approximately 25% of transactions in the US, as I argue that as the credit card balance continues to rise, in my opinion, regulation or some type of limit is inevitable, which is negative sentiment for Mastercard, as gross dollar volume will be affected as consumers might start spending less. Other measures like capping interest rates may start to take place as Senator Hawley advocates it, which doesn't affect Mastercard, but more extreme measures could affect gross dollar volumes, which could affect Mastercard.

Mastercard is one of the firms likely at the center of attention by regulators. Mastercard has a wide moat, as without Mastercard payment processing systems (and Visa), the world today would be impossible to operate if there were no payment processing system. It continues to face continuous scrutiny as catalysts are minimal. If regulation does start to increase and gross dollar volume does show signs of slowing down, Mastercard's revenue faces significant headwind as its revenue is in its payment network.

Mastercard is not Saturated yet

Mastercard continues to get more and more saturated as there are minimal growth catalysts except for increased consumer spending, which is already a problem in the US. The amount of Mastercard cards only increased by 6%, which isn't that impressive with revenues up 16% and a valuation 3x of the sector median.

Use of card payments with Mastercard isn't fully saturated yet, as many locations in the world like South East Asia are starting to adopt card/digital payments. Places like Asia Pacific, the Middle East and Africa (APMEA) are growing at faster rates than the US where Mastercard is already established, as APMEA grew 21% in 2 years whilst the US grew only 13.97%. Even though Europe is Mastercard's biggest overseas segment, it grew by 26.6% over 2 years, which shows that the use of card payments isn't all saturated yet, as consumers are still shifting to card payments. However, if the purchase transactions show signs of slowing down, such as only increasing at the inflation rate or the % increase in disposable income, it shows that there are no new adopters of cards that use Mastercard.

Easing Regulation also affects Mastercard

GENIUS Act allows the existence of stablecoins, which is a cryptocurrency that are backed by assets that are considered reliable. A key example is Circle's (CRCL) USDC, which is 1:1 to the US dollar.

Mass adoption hasn't started yet and I'm not saying that using stablecoins as payment will be the norm with a society switch from card to stablecoins, but the existence and acceptance of stablecoins being passed by the senate threatens Mastercard's business model, as it avoids the whole Mastercard network. As long as President Trump is President, further easing of regulations of digital currency will happen as the fear will continue to linger for Mastercard in my opinion. This fear will continue in Trump's tenure, and the fear will only go away if mass adoption (or a % of society) doesn't occur in the short term, assuming the next president is not pro-Crypto. An example is Trump trying to push non-traditional assets like Crypto as a possibility to be invested into individuals' retirement savings, which I believe shows his push towards the adoption of Crypto. If the popularity of stablecoins increases or forms of tokenization, Mastercard's business model is directly under fire.

Cryptocurrency like Bitcoin isn't losing popularity either. A recent survey from Deloitte with North American CFOs showed some very interesting opinions of the executives. 1% did not envision using cryptocurrency for business functions in the long-term, organizations with revenue upwards of $10B had 40% of their CFOs believe that their treasury department will utilize crypto for investments/payments within 2 years, with the main concern for the executives being price volatility.

Industry regulation and security were huge concerns, being 40% and 36%, which are also huge concerns in the general population as crypto is not yet believed to be safe or reliable as it is new to society, with a survey in 2024 showing that 63% of Americans have ways to invest, use or trade currency in ways that are safe/reliable. Despite fears, US cryptocurrency payment users continue to rise, estimating a jump of 82% from 2024 to 2026.

The main takeaway is that crypto adoption is not slowing down, the bottom line being in the US, as the US wants to lead the world by being the first to mass adopt crypto as payments rather than just another tradable security. As more and more users adopt crypto, it gets more and more likely that payments will start shifting towards crypto. This directly affects Mastercard's gross dollar volume, though the extent cannot be known yet with crypto adoption just starting, it will affect Mastercard's growth as crypto completely avoids Mastercard's network.

With the US being at 30.9% of the gross dollar volume, the threat of crypto is very real and will affect Mastercard's revenue as it is a significant portion of its revenue. If crypto adoption in the US is successful, there are also possibilities that other countries will also start to adopt crypto, as a key advantage is sending money abroad, since Mastercard's network would have fees or payment delays/lag by the issuer. Time will tell whether crypto will be a huge threat to Mastercard's networks and revenue, but it is seemingly shifting that way.

Mastercard's Valuation and Index Comparison

This is Mastercard's Valuation Metrics:

From a valuation standpoint in its relative sector, Mastercard is overvalued from a comparison based on the numbers. However, investors have to remember that Mastercard has a moat over its sector, and it essentially runs a duopoly with Visa. It deserves a high P/E and a premium to the sector as it is essentially 1 of 2 firms who have networks globally/worldwide to connect the consumer to the merchant, as it is unreasonable to compare Mastercard to its sector when only 2 firms have such dominance in the world in payment processing, which are Visa and Mastercard. However, I would argue that Mastercard is slightly overvalued compared to its peer Visa.

Here is a comparison of P/E GAAP with Mastercard and its peers:

Here is a comparison of P/S with Mastercard and its peers:

The key trend is set, that Visa and Mastercard tend to trade at a deserving premium to their sector, as it trades at a premium to the only other payment processing system that has a considerable market share, American Express. Both firms have similar moats, as they both heavily rely on the payment network system, which is evident from the heavily similar P/E and P/S.

On a P/E basis, Mastercard would have to fall by ~14% to get to Visa's P/E GAAP basis. On a non-GAAP basis, it would have to fall by ~17% to get to Visa's P/E non-GAAP level. On a P/S basis, Mastercard would have to fall by around ~7% to get to Visa's level.

I'd argue that the premium of Visa and Mastercard to American Express and PayPal or payment firms is fully justified, as their moat extends globally and is relied upon daily by consumers and merchants. However, I argue that the bottom line is that Mastercard doesn't deserve a premium over Visa. Visa expects net revenue growth of high single digits to low double digits, while Mastercard's net revenue is expected to grow at a low teens rate. This doesn't justify an overvaluation of ~7%-~17% depending on the metric compared. This may also signal that Visa is undervalued, but Mastercard is already overvalued by 3x to the relative sector, which is quite significant. Mastercard is also trading at slightly higher levels than its average and median P/E, which sits currently at ~37.15.

Mastercard is a compounding machine, but the growth rate has to be questioned for long-term holders. I'm cherry picking the metric that Mastercard has underperformed the S&P500 on a 5Y scale, despite outperforming on any other long-term scale. It shows that further compounding for Mastercard may be foggy, considering the huge risk it is facing today. It outperformed as revenue grew as fast as card adoption was accelerating, but it is already quite saturated now as the next shift is towards crypto, which Mastercard has no big part of. It performed nearly 2x on a 10Y basis, but questions have to be raised as card adoption is slowing down due to saturation and a shift to crypto will affect gross dollar volumes, which will affect Mastercard in the long term.

Conclusion

Mastercard will continue to grow as long as spending with cards through Mastercard's networks continues to be the norm in society, but the risk of crypto is only getting bigger and bigger every day that Trump is president. For a long-term hold, Mastercard is not a bad hold, as it is highly unlikely that globally people will just stop using cards that have Mastercard's network, but the risk of easing or tighter regulation both affecting Mastercard from different angles is only getting larger every day. With limited upside as it is unlikely that Mastercard gets a higher valuation, being a lot higher than Visa's valuations and already higher than its historical valuations, Mastercard remains at a 'Hold'.

data.title: The 2 Biggest Dividend Stock Bargains I See In The Berkshire Hathaway Portfolio
data.description:
I have been using the stock market rally to sell into strength, but I still see pockets of opportunity in a couple of sectors and also with a number of select stocks. I believe the market could be forming a top right now, and that recession risks are higher than average. All of this means that the bar is higher right now when it comes to investing and for that reason, a stock has to be a very compelling value for me to buy into it. Some of the reasons why I am concerned with the stock market and the economy in general include high valuations, and my belief that the full impact of tariffs has yet to be felt. I'm also concerned with the latest jobs data which shows a weakening trend may have just started.

Warren Buffett also seems to be cautious these days and he has built up a massive cash hoard at Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A), (BRK.B), which is just over $344 billion, as of June 30, 2025. Buffett is known to be a disciplined investor and he uses something known as the "Buffett Indicator" as part of his investment discipline. This indicator takes the total value of the U.S. stock market and divides it by the Gross Domestic Product or "GDP". The current estimated value of the U.S. stock market is around $60 trillion and the U.S. GDP is nearly $30 trillion. This indicates a 200% ratio of market value to GDP, which is historically very high. The chart below also shows that the current valuation is 57% above the long-term trend.

Even though I see a number of potential warning signs for the stock market right now which includes the fact that Buffett has a very large amount of cash on hand and that the Buffett Indicator shows valuations are stretched, I still think it makes sense to buy stocks that appear to be deeply undervalued. Right now there are two dividend stocks in the Berkshire Hathaway portfolio that I feel are too cheap to ignore. I like that Warren Buffett appears to see significant value in these stocks and I'm also interested in these stocks because they are relatively recession-resistant. With this in mind, let's take a closer look.

Constellation Brands

Constellation Brands (STZ) is a producer, importer and marketer of a number of well-known wine and beer brands such as Corona Light, Modelo, Pacifico, Robert Mondavi and more. This company has been challenged recently by tariffs as well as an apparent shift in alcohol consumption. I think these concerns are worth considering, but it also seems like these challenges might be overblown, so this could be a buying opportunity. The 52-week high for this stock is around $261, and the stock is now trading for just about $160 per share today. Berkshire Hathaway owns around 13.4 million shares with a value of more than $2 billion.

The Chart

Shares of Constellation Brands are trading below the 50-day moving average, which is about $167 and the 200-day moving average, which is around $185. This stock appears to have hit a bottom at around $160 more than once and this could be a sign of a bullish multiple bottom and a strong support level for the stock.

Earnings Estimates And The Balance Sheet

As the earnings estimates (provided by Seeking Alpha) below show, this stock is trading for about 12 times earnings. When you consider that the S&P 500 Index (SPY) is trading for about 27 times earnings, it suggests this stock is potentially deeply undervalued.

Earnings Estimates

As for the balance sheet, this company has about $11.57 billion in debt and around $73.9 million in cash.

The Dividend And Share Buybacks

STZ pays a quarterly dividend of $1.02 per share, for a total of $4.08 annually. On April 9, 2025, it announced a $4 billion share buyback authorization. With the market cap currently at about $28 billion, a $4 billion buyback could be significant, if it is completed.

Potential Risk Factors

Alcohol consumption has been declining, and it seems to be less popular with younger generations that are more health conscious. RFK Jr. has been focused on making Americans more healthy and his policies have already impacted some food stocks and the alcohol segment could be next. I don't believe we have seen the full impact of tariffs yet and this could hurt financial results going forward. Tariffs of 50% on aluminum and steel products were recently announced and this could impact the business.

In Summary

There are consumption and tariff risks for Constellation Brands, but I believe these concerns have already been more than priced into the stock since it is trading for a big discount when compared to the PE ratio for the market overall. I also believe that margin pressures from tariffs could be mitigated by price increases. Tariff policies could also change under a new administration in 2028. As for a potential trend showing a decline in alcohol consumption, this sector might be able to offset a decline in consumption with price increases. This is what tobacco companies have done for years and the alcohol industry is facing a much slower rate of decline in consumption. Alcohol consumption typically remains solid in recessions, and that means this stock could be recession-resistant. I also feel that since this stock has bottomed out at around $160 more than once this year, it could be a sign that this stock has hit rock-bottom levels and is now worth buying for a potential rebound.

UnitedHealth Group

UnitedHealth Group (UNH) is a recent stock purchase for Berkshire Hathaway which now owns just over five million shares currently valued at more than $1.5 billion. This comes at an interesting time because this stock has been cut in half since the April highs of about $600 per share. The company has been in focus and under pressure for several months for multiple issues including profit margin pressures, executive turnover, and investigations.

The Chart

As the chart below shows, this stock hit a low of around $240 per share in August, but it rebounded after Berkshire Hathaway disclosed a new stake in this company. This stock is now trading above the 50-day moving average which is around $288. However, it is still trading way below the 200-day moving average which is around $427. Furthermore, with the stock at just around $300 today, it has been cut in half from the highs in April.

Earnings Estimates And The Balance Sheet

As the earnings estimates (provided by Seeking Alpha) below show, this stock is trading for about 18 times earnings estimates for 2025. However, these are potentially trough earnings since earnings are expected to rebound significantly in 2026 and 2027, largely due to expected price increases in policy premiums.

Earnings Estimates

FY

EPS

YoY

PE

Sales

YoY

As for the balance sheet, this company has about $79.19 billion in debt and around $32 billion in cash.

The Dividend

UNH pays a quarterly dividend of $2.21 per share, for a total of $8.84 annually. This provides a yield of nearly 3%.

Here Are Some Positive Factors To Consider With UnitedHealth

I believe the market reaction of essentially cutting the value of this stock in half since April is overdone. This is based on past examples of Department of Justice criminal investigations of companies like Boeing (BA) and Chipotle (CMG) which both went through this process, saw their share price decline and then saw the stock rebound as settlements were announced with the DOJ. Chipotle ended a criminal DOJ investigation over food safety issues by agreeing to a deferred prosecution agreement and by paying a $25 million criminal fine. Boeing was able to resolve issues it had on May 29, 2025, when the DOJ filed to dismiss the complaint because it reached a non-prosecution agreement with the company. I believe this shows the most likely outcome is for UnitedHealth to eventually pay a fine if there is any wrongdoing and this will settle the case and let the company move on.

As for the slide in recent profits, It appears that insurance companies like UnitedHealth are planning to raise prices in 2026, and this could lead to the expected profit rebound in the coming years.

In addition to the recently disclosed stock purchases by Berkshire Hathaway, multiple insiders including the CEO have been buying this beaten down stock. The CEO, Stephen Hemsley purchased 86,700 shares of UnitedHealth in May for about $288 per share, and the CFO, John Rex bought over 17,000 shares.

Another positive about UnitedHealth is that this stock has no tariff risks and it is a recession-resistant business since we all need health insurance even in tough times.

Potential Risk Factors

In an SEC filing, UnitedHealth disclosed that the Department of Justice or "DOJ" was investigating its Medicare program billing practices. However, this investigation has reportedly expanded to include other practices at the company. I believe more negative headlines could be coming over the next few months and this could keep a lid on the stock or even put renewed selling pressure on it. However, the significant amount of stock purchased by insiders and Berkshire Hathaway appear to be a vote of confidence and a sign that UnitedHealth is likely to get past these challenges in the long run.

In Summary

UnitedHealth has challenges, but the challenges all appear to be resolvable. While the market overall appears overvalued, there are pockets of opportunities and I believe it makes sense to follow Warren Buffett into these two dividend stocks which appear undervalued. I would add that I only plan to buy these stocks in stages so that I can average in and build a position over time.

data.title: It's Not Just AI -- The 100 P/E For SoFi Is Also A Bubble (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Thesis Summary

SoFi Technologies, Inc. (NASDAQ:SOFI) is one of the hottest names in fintech right now. This is understandable after it posted a really strong Q2 2025.

SoFi has captured the younger generation and is becoming their bank of choice. There’s clearly a lot of potential for SoFi to keep growing and increasing its profitability.

But does this mean the current valuation makes sense? With a P/E of over 100, SoFi is entering bubble territory.

I last wrote about SoFi back in Q3 of 2024, when the stock traded at just over $10. The stock has now doubled and surpassed my initial technical targets.

I am compelled to take some profits in SoFi given the overextended technicals and valuation.

Q2 Was Great, That’s Undeniable

First, credit where credit is due. SoFi delivered another quarter of very strong results.

For the quarter, we saw strong growth in the lending segment, up 30%. Financial services were up over 100% YoY, and revenues from the technology platform increased 15% YoY.

SoFi beat expectations and also provided some pretty bullish guidance.

SoFi is looking to achieve over $3.3 billion in revenues by the end of 2025, with Adjusted EBITDA jumping to almost $1 billion.

The engine of growth remains its lending segment, which accounts for nearly half of total revenue, but the more exciting growth is coming from financial services.

Clearly, SoFi is executing. The issue isn’t performance; it’s valuation.

Does This Valuation Make Sense?

This is where things get tricky. At current levels, SoFi trades at a P/E of over 100. Let’s compare that to some of its fintech peers.

SOFI

UPST

HOOD

PYPL

P/E Non-GAAP (FY1)

82,80

44,66

55,28

13,45

P/E Non-GAAP (FY2)

49,95

29,66

49,23

12,20

Revenue Growth (YoY)

29,27%

53,62%

59,38%

4,07%

Revenue Growth (FWD)

26,48%

37,70%

36,71%

5,60%

Gross Profit Margin

82,25%

81,17%

91,51%

41,68%

Data Source: SA.

I think we have some pretty comparable stocks to SoFi above:

  • Upstart (UPST): Providing similar lending services, though less of a bank perhaps.

  • Robinhood (HOOD): A stockbroker first, but slowly gaining traction with other financial services.

  • PayPal (PYPL): A veteran in the sector, but arguably also a close competitor.

The growth and margins of these companies are also quite similar, with the exception of PayPal.

Robinhood and Upstart are projected to grow more than SoFi next year, and HOOD also has a higher gross profit margin.

But SoFi clearly is the most expensive of the bunch, even if we look at FY2 estimates.

It’s similarly priced to Robinhood in some ways, but that doesn’t mean the valuation makes sense either. Plus, Robinhood may be in a better position to gain market share.

Meanwhile, companies like Upstart, PayPal and even other traditional banks offer exposure to the growing and exciting world of fintech, without having to pay such a high price.

Technical Analysis

SoFi has seen a relentless rally, and it is time for a pull-back.

We are way overbought, even on the weekly chart, and we have arguably formed a bearish divergence in the RSI.

Also, we’re approaching the post IPO top, which could prove an important level of resistance.

Other Considerations

The way I see it, SoFi is a great company, and it deserves a premium valuation. However, it has been caught up in some of the recent market euphoria.

This is a stock that is beloved by retail investors, and it also has a relatively high short interest, over 9%, which can partially help explain why it has rallied so hard.

It’s not just AI stocks that have rallied hard in 2025. We’re now seeing all the usual high-risk plays begin to take off. Companies with high short interest, meme stocks, cryptocurrencies and even some Chinese stocks.

In this regard, it’s important to consider that SoFi may very well keep rallying hard if this bullish sentiment prevails.

The price is detached from fundamentals, and that could keep going on for some time. Nonetheless, we must consider the potential risk and reward right now.

Other factors that could help this rally continue could be lower yields and rate cuts, especially aggressive rate cuts if Trump can gain influence over the Fed in 2026.

Final Thoughts

At $10, SoFi Technologies, Inc. stock looked like a no-brainer, but we’ve doubled in less than a year, and I think it's time to take some profits.

It’s a good company in a good industry, but also a very competitive one.

Robinhood, for example, is a big threat and a company I would prefer to own right now.

They are similarly valued, but I think Robinhood has more potential.

data.title: SEALSQ: Why The Valuation Is Finally Rational
data.description:
SEALSQ Corp (NASDAQ:LAES) is worth a revisit here, following the cautious Hold rating three months ago when I last covered the stock here on Seeking Alpha. The cautious stance seems to have aged well, as the stock is down about 24% since that coverage. In this revisit, I'll look at the company’s progress from both an operational and market perspective, drawing insights from both fundamental and operational metrics. The goal is not to catch the absolute LAES bottom, but to identify an entry where risk and reward align better for investors.

For investors who are not familiar with the name SEALSQ Corp: it is a quantum company that is a subsidiary of the Swiss cybersecurity firm WISeKey International Holding (WKEY). SEALSQ is a pure-play technology firm focused on developing secure semiconductors, Public Key Infrastructure [PKI], and post-quantum technology hardware and software products.

SEALSQ has already developed post-quantum tech that have seen real-world implementation. For example, SEALSQ’s SEALCOIN post-quantum chips have been launched with WiseKey's WISeSat nanosatellite into orbit via SpaceX. As a subsidiary of WiseKey, SEALSQ leverages many of WiseKey's strategic partnerships and assets as an enabler to its growth. Another example of SEALSQ’s technology implementation is the QS7001 secure hardware platform, which is currently being tested to quantum-proof blockchain platforms like Hedera (HBAR-USD). The company boasts over 25 years of expertise in semiconductor secure chip design and has seen its technology implemented in over 1.6 billion devices globally. So the pivot to post-quantum chips comes naturally for the company. There has been a historic inventory glut for traditional chip demand generally and demand has shifted to AI and purpose-built specific chips. Thus, SEALSQ’s pivot to post-quantum chip design is perfectly timed.

Lately, quantum companies have started moving beyond research and into product releases for early-stage use. The sector is now shifting from prototypes and MoUs to practical applications of quantum technology. Sales are shaping up among emerging quantum companies with real products and use cases.

We’ve seen quantum-focused companies like IonQ (IONQ) report $20.7 million in revenue for Q2 2025, which was a ~170% YoY increase, driven by a large commercial deal for the Forte Enterprise system. D-Wave Quantum (QBTS) reported a jump of over 500% to record $15 million in sales in Q1, propelled by a large one-time commercial sale of the Advantage-1 computer system to a client in Germany. Though D-Wave’s Q2 top line was down sequentially due to the Q1 one-time sale, it was still a 40.9% increase YoY. Subsequent launch of D-Wave’s Advantage-2 computing system in Q2 saw QBTS leap higher in stock price. The point I’m stressing is that the market responds strongly to product releases of these quantum stocks, and sales are also shifting from smaller, one-off research-based projects to multi-million dollar, long-term commercial deployment

I believe the stage is set for SEALSQ also, and the Q4 target products release and customer sampling, and then commercialization (target of Q4 2025 to Q1 2026) of its QS7001 platform and the QVault TPM modules are the catalysts in the meantime. SEALSQ successfully completed the cryptographic toolbox validation stage for the QS7001 platform last month and has formally begun the Common Criteria certification process for both the QS7001 platform and the QVault TPM module. Common Criteria EAL5+ certification is expected to be achieved sometime next year.

An analysis of SEALSQ's operations shows a confluence of factors creating a perfect inflection point and timing for a potential breakout in Q4, which would also likely propel the rest of the fiscal year's target sales. If SEALSQ’s FY25 target sales of $16 million to $20 million are met, it would imply a 45% to 82% YoY jump. Such headline top line growth holds the potential to drive a near-term re-rating for the stock.

SEALSQ’s Path to Sustained Sales and Premium Valuation

In the meantime, I think there isn't much to extract from the financials of companies like SEALSQ beyond how the sales pipeline is shaping up and how feasible the sales targets are. The H1 2025 report (released last month) was less about historical performance and more about providing forward-looking guidance and reassuring investors that the growth catalysts are still on track.

Revenue remained flat in H1 2025 because the company's focus has shifted from legacy products (traditional semiconductors and PKI) to next-gen semiconductors and software solutions (post-quantum chips). Near-term liquidity looks very strong, as current assets of $93.1 million exceed current liabilities of $15.5 million. SEALSQ’s cash position also jumped to $120 million in H1, up from $19 million a year ago and $85 million as of 2024 end. The cash increase is a result of $9.15 million from warrant exercises that were converted successfully in early January, then a follow-on equity offering of $20 million in May, that has provided the company with refreshed cash runway.

Growing bookings is an important barometer to assess a company like SEALSQ. The company confirmed in the H1 report that current business pipeline now stands at $145 million of revenue opportunities with both current and prospective clients for the period 2026 to 2028, of which $6.8 million of confirmed bookings was already secured as of Q1 this year.

SEALSQ's bottom line still seems miles away from profitability as the net loss totaled $21.2 million in FY24. Most of these emerging quantum companies are still grappling with high costs, mainly in R&D and sales and marketing. Investing in them is basically a bet on the future, where risk equals reward. Their success hinges greatly on the commercialization of new technology.

SEALSQ's product launch timeline is strategically aligning with the company’s strategic acquisitions. SEALSQ has been actively engaging in acquisitions as a key part of its growth strategy. They have made some full or minority strategic investments in a series of tech companies this year, and the style of acquisition appears to be highly strategic and focused on creating both vertical and technology integration and synergy. The company acquires businesses that complement and enhance their core technological capabilities, especially in the post-quantum and secure semiconductor space.

SEALSQ has completed the acquisition of IC'ALPS, a French specialist in Application-Specific Integrated Circuits [ASIC] design. The acquisition was pending French government approval at the time of the H1 report but has since been finalized. The deal was for a fixed purchase price of €12.5 million, payable in a combination of cash and LAES shares, with a potential earn-out of up to €4 million contingent on IC'ALPS achieving revenue in excess of €11 million for full year 2025. I think that target for IC’ALPs isn't out of reach, as the company reported €9.76 million in revenue for the full year 2024. The consolidation of revenue from IC'ALPS is expected to be a major contributor to SEALSQ’s 2H 2025 revenue surge, thereby helping SEALSQ reach the full-year target $16 million to $20 million.

Looking beyond the immediate impact, the acquisition is not just about immediate revenue increment. It is a strategic move to combine SEALSQ's post-quantum IP with IC'ALPS's ASIC design expertise to create new, high-demand products for the future, which is why the earn-out is tied to IC'ALPS's future performance. This shows that SEALSQ's management has confidence in the long-term synergy of the deal, even if the short-term impact is simply a revenue lift. The IC'ALPS acquisition has the potential to become accretive to the bottom line in the long term because of the combination of revenue and cost synergies that are expected to materialize over time, but it may not be immediately accretive at the moment because IC'ALPS itself is running at a net loss prior to acquisition, reporting net loss of €2.02 million. Investing in LAES would therefore be a long-term play.

The sales and enterprise value valuations of LAES are now more rational compared to late last year and early this year when the quantum hype was in full swing. Several so-called quantum companies with no revenue to show were trading at sales and EV multiples over 1000x at the time. There has been a marked derating since, and quantum stocks without an identifiable revenue model have seen steep corrections.

SEALSQ has moved from the hype-driven bucket of pre-revenue peers, but revenue still seems paltry compared to the company’s lofty promising bookings figures. At current $408 million market cap, $2.70 stock price, and $11 million in trailing twelve month sales, LAES now trades at a more rational valuation of around 37x P/Sales on a trailing twelve-month basis.

As we have established thus far, the revenue targets for FY25 are not unattainable, especially since there will be consolidation of revenue with the acquired companies including IC’ALPS. If the lower end of $16 million revenue is reached, at the current $2.70 stock price LAES will be trading around 26x forward P/S. Compared to peers like IONQ and QBTS with forward P/S at 138x and 220x respectively, LAES is trading on the conservative side.

Risks

With all the positive developments highlighted so far, the quantum sector still remains in its infancy. While SEALSQ exudes enough confidence in its forward-looking bookings, there are still a number of risks. Firstly, note that the company is currently operating at a significant net loss, which totaled $21.2 million in FY24. While there is a strong cash position, SEALSQ is not yet generating profits and the cash runway depends on its ability to convert pipeline into profitable revenue.

Then there is the commercialization risk, which the near-term potential rerating hinges a lot on. The post-quantum market is still emerging. There is no guarantee that SEALSQ's new technologies will be adopted quickly enough to justify its high R&D and acquisition costs. On top of that, revenue from its new quantum products is not expected until at least Q4 2025 or more likely in Q1 2026.

Takeaway

SEALSQ is a high-risk, high-reward quantum play that has moved beyond the "hype" stage. With its valuation now more rational, the company's success hinges on a handful of near-term catalysts - primarily, the timely commercialization of new post-quantum products and the successful revenue consolidation from acquisitions.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Enterprise Products Partners: Insiders Bought More In July, So Did I
data.description:
EPD stock: July insider activities in focus

I last covered Enterprise Products Partners L.P. (NYSE:EPD) on July 8 with an article entitled " Why Enterprise Products Partners Is A Stronger Dividend Stock Than ExxonMobil.” That article focused on EPD’s dividends and rated it as a Strong Buy. Since then, a new catalyst that caught my attention was its latest insider activities. These activities appear very unusual to me and triggered this full re-examination of the stock. In the end, I believe the insider purchases reflect a highly skewed reward/risk ratio in the stock and thus reiterate my Strong Buy rating.

More specifically, the next chart shows the insider transactions I was referring to. As seen, in July, there were two new purchases disclosure. These purchases totaled close to $1 million. The most recent purchase was disclosed on July 30, 2025, when 16,000 shares were purchased at a stock price of $31.55 by director Montgomery. A day earlier on July 29, 15,000 shares were purchased at a stock price of $31.34 by director Rutherford. Before these latest activities, the last batch of insider purchase was made Co-CEO Teague about 1 year ago in August 2024. These insider transactions piqued my interest for several reasons as elaborated on next.

EPD stock: why the insider activities stand out

First and foremost, the pattern of EPD’s insider transactions is very unusual in my eyes. There were no insider selling in reported in the past 2 years as you can teel from the screenshot above. This is in stark contrast to most of they other companies that I follow, where insider transactions were dominated by selling. The equity market as whole is also dominated by insider selling activities per the data provided below.

Secondly, the prices of the insiders’ purchases are also interesting to me. Note that the latest insider purchases were reported at a share price of ~$31 and the co-CEO’s purchases in 2024 were disclosure at a price of ~$29. These purchase prices all corresponded to a P/E ratio of around 11x with the EPS at the times when they were made. As to be explained later, I do believe an entry P/E of 11x provides a very favorable reward/risk profile under current conditions.

EPD stock: long-term growth prospects

Overall, I am optimistic about EPD long-term growth prospects, especially with the Trump administration’s emphasis on domestic energy production. EPD is well positioned for a crucial role here. As the largest member in this space, EPD has a presence in just about every major American shale play. Enterprise has also been investing large sums in capital projects in recent years, and I expect these investments to pay dividends – pun intended – for years to come. Over the past two years, the capital budget has averaged nearly $4.0 billion annually. For 2025, leadership estimates that funds used on growth projects ought to be in the neighborhood of $4.0 billion to $4.5 billion in 2025 before falling to $2.0 billion to $2.5 billion in 2026.

Assuming a growth investment of $2.25 billion (the mid-point of the guidance range), the reinvestment rate (RR) would be about 26% going forward even at this level. Such an RR, when combined with my projected ROCE (return on capital employed), could support a health growth sustainably. My method for projection long-term growth rate is detailed in this earlier article and here I will only quote the end results:

The method involves the return on capital employed (“ROCE”) and RR. The ROCE for EPD has been around 13.2% in recent years as seen in the chart below and I expect its ROCE to reach about 15% in the next few years as CAPEX spendings level off. With an estimated RR of 26%, its organic growth rate would be about 4% (15% ROCE x 26% RR = 3.9%).

Investors should not be under impressed by a growth rate of 4% at all for reasons to be explained next.

EPD stock: valuation and growth

Firstly, the growth rate projected by the above approach is the real growth rate. To obtain the notional growth rates we often quote, an escalation factor needs to be added and can push the notional growth rate higher.

Secondly, with the current P/E ratio for EPD, even a 4% growth rate could lead into robust total return potential in the longer term. As a reflection of its total return potential, let’s consider the PEGY ratio that Peter Lynch promoted for the following reason:

A revised version of the PEG ratio, the PEGY ratio is defined as the P/E ratio divided by the sum of the earnings growth rate and dividend yield. The idea behind the PEGY is very simple and effective (most effective ideas are simple). If a stock pays out a large part of its earnings as dividends, then investors do not need a high growth rate to enjoy healthy returns. And vice versa. And similar to the PEG ratio, his preference is a PEGY ratio of 1x or below.

In the case of EPD, its current FWD P/E ratio is 11.9x as seen in the chart below. Note that its current P/E ratio represents a discount relative to similar stocks. For instance, peers (like ET, KMI, and WMS) trades with an FWD ranging from about 13x to almost 27x. EPS provides a FWD dividend yield of 6.82% currently. Even with a 4% growth rate projection, the PEGY ratio worked out to be about 1.1x, quote close to the idea threshold of 1x.

Other risks and final thoughts

Before closing, a few other risks, both in the upside and downside directions are worth nothing. Energy stocks, especially pipe-line stocks, are debt-heavy. As such, stocks in this sector are not only sensitive to commodity price changes but also more sensitive to interest rates uncertainties than the overall economy. EPD is no exception. As an example, EPD's debt-to-equity ratio is more than 110%. As you can see from the table below, this is not unusual in the sector. Its close peers all feature similarly high leverage ratios. The debt-to-equity ratio is 134% for ET, 192% for WMB, and 102% for KMI.

As a compensation, Enterprise is well diversified both geographically and operationally. As aforementioned, EPD is involved in almost every shale play and does business in every segment of the midstream industry. Thanks to such diversification, the business is more resilient compared to its peers in more view and exhibited less cyclicality than almost all of its peers historically. As a reflection of such resilience, during the past 10 years, many companies in the industry were forced to cut dividends for various reasons (see the next chart below). But Enterprise not only didn’t cut its distribution, but also kept growing its payouts (for 27 consecutive years thus far).

To conclude, this follow-up article of EPD is mainly triggered by the disclosure of its insider activities in July 2025. Insiders’ purchases in July piqued my interest for several reasons and triggered a full reexamination of its growth, profitability, and valuations. My conclusion is that, at the entry P/E (around 11x) of these insider purchases, the stock indeed provides far more upside potential than downside risks.

data.title: WTI Crude Steadies Near As Traders Weigh Demand Slowdown Against Supply Risks
data.description:
By Jainam Mehta

WTI crude oil (CL1:COM) is stabilizing after a bruising August that saw prices tumble from above $71 to near $62 before recovering into the $64 zone. On Friday, futures hovered at $64.30, extending a two-week rebound but still on course for the first monthly loss since the spring rally.

The technical shift from a clear downtrend to a rising recovery channel underscores a market in transition, where bearish fundamentals are being counterbalanced by supportive headlines.

WTI price dynamics (Source: TradingView)

On the four-hour chart, WTI now trades between $62 at the channel base and $66 at the ceiling. The cluster of moving averages around $64 reflects indecision, while the 200-period EMA near $64.68 remains the key tactical pivot. A sustained close above that level would be the first sign in weeks that bulls are regaining meaningful control. Momentum has improved, with RSI at 60 after climbing steadily from below 40 earlier this month, but conviction remains limited until resistance gives way.

Demand worries collide with supply risks

Fundamentals explain the hesitation. U.S. gasoline demand typically peaks at Labor Day, after which refineries lower runs for maintenance. The International Energy Agency recently forecast that global supply will outpace consumption in coming quarters, and the gradual return of OPEC+ production reinforces that view. These dynamics keep upside capped, with rallies meeting profit-taking as traders price in weaker autumn demand.

Yet the market has avoided a deeper breakdown. Rising geopolitical risks, particularly Ukrainian strikes on Russian export infrastructure, have added a risk premium, while talk of tighter sanctions enforcement by Washington continues to circulate. India’s appetite for Russian barrels also remains a wildcard, as purchases are expected to rise in September despite U.S. pressure. These forces have prevented bears from driving WTI decisively below $62.

Technical roadmap for September

Key levels remain well defined. Resistance sits at $65 and then $66 at the top of the rising channel. A daily close above $66, paired with stronger momentum, would likely open a run toward $68 and potentially $70, where the early August breakdown zone rests. Support is layered at $63.50 to $63.00, with the channel base at $62 serving as a critical test. A breakdown there would risk reopening $60.50 to $61, an area that previously attracted heavy buying.

Previously, analysis noted that August’s slide had left WTI vulnerable unless buyers could reclaim key moving averages. That observation remains relevant. While momentum has recovered and price action is constructive inside a rising channel, the market still requires a decisive breakout above $66 to shift sentiment firmly. Until then, oil remains range-bound, with traders leaning on technical edges while monitoring macro and geopolitical catalysts.

This material may contain third-party opinions, none of the data and information on this webpage constitutes investment advice according to our Disclaimer. While we adhere to strict Editorial Integrity, this post may contain references to products from our partners.

Original Post

data.title: Clarus: DTC Worrying Bellwether For Consumer Response To Price Hikes
data.description:
Clarus Corporation (NASDAQ:CLAR) has some tariff exposure, probably around 6% of annualized operating expenses, and its price increases that we discussed in the previous coverage have only now been taken up by retailers after the close of the quarter. Negative mix effects from adventure are persisting from the inventory that they are trying to get rid of and they are about halfway done with clearing that. The DTC channel where the effects of the price increases are clearer, showed significant weakness. We would focus on this threat for the moment and see towards the end of the year when the profitability ends up being, also considering outstanding questions around tariffs.

Comments on results

There are a couple of things that concern us. The company was trying to eliminate lower-margin products and inventory at the beginning of the year. Some of that is still weighing on results, including soft demand from OEMs in adventure, though also emerging attempts to find new markets in underpenetrated geographies like the Americas. But now they are looking to reduce promotionality, and they also have to mitigate tariffs.

At Adventure, most of -- they source everything from China with a little bit coming from Australia -- made in region in Australia, okay? For Black Diamond, the majority of our inventory comes from Southeast Asia. I'm looking for the exact piece here: 25% from China, 31% from Taiwan, 15% from Vietnam, and 12% from the Philippines, with the remainder coming from elsewhere.

Michael J Yates, CFO of CLAR.

What concerns us is that the expectation for around $4 million in an annual tariff hit is based on assumptions that are now outdated. (At least there is an offset from layoffs generating $1 million in annual savings in the adventure segment as it's downsized).

However, looking ahead for the year, we expect the current tariffs, which include the 10% reciprocal tariffs on most countries, 50% on steel and aluminum and 55% on China to have a $3.4 million impact on earnings even after our mitigating measures. And of course, we are all waiting to see how the reciprocal tariff situation plays out. Should these tariffs land above the current 10% level, we expect to see an additional drag on earnings in 2025.

McNeil Seymour Fiske, Black Diamond President.

Tariffs are significantly ahead of the 10% mark for most of the trade deals at this point. Crudely, if the annual tariff impact was around 4% of annualized expenses before, with higher reciprocal tariffs it may be closer to 6% which is quite significant. More than expected pressure from tariffs is our first direct worry.

The price increases they are doing to offset these tariff impacts may really hit results. The business is primarily wholesale-based, and the price increases in the wholesale channel have not yet fully taken effect.

And while they didn't all take prices up immediately, that extra bit of time gives them time to plan, adjust and also confirm their fall 2025 orders. Whereas now, I think, with a lot of our peer group, they've taken prices up in July, and there's quite a bit of commotion now in the wholesale channel about how to adjust to those price changes right at the beginning of the season.

McNeil Seymour Fiske.

What worries us is the DTC softness where the planned price increases were quicker to implement because there are no middle steps like distributors or retailers.

From a strategy standpoint, we have moved D2C even more aggressively to a full price position. And we rolled out prices early -- new prices early in North America on D2C. And so the combination of less discounting and less promotion and higher prices, we saw a pretty sharp pullback in the D2C channel, 20% in North America, a little bit more modest in Europe.

McNeil Seymour Fiske.

We think there could be more downside for the wholesale sales as well. DTC is around 20% of the NA revenues in outdoor, which is the segment that is dominated by Black Diamond. All the tariff impacts are focused on this segment since Adventure has most of its markets outside the U.S. So far Black Diamond revenues are up 2% from the prior year, with NA up around 1.6% dragged down by the DTC component. Margins were up around 80 bps in Black Diamond to 34.9% and would have been higher if it hadn't been for FX hedging. Now with the disposal of the PIEPS business, still a source of legal issues, EBITDA is in positive territory on an adjusted basis for the outdoor segment.

Second quarter sales were $55.2 million compared to $56.5 million in the prior year's second quarter. The 2% decline in total sales was driven by the decrease in the Adventure segment of 8% and an increase in the Outdoor Segment of 1%. FX was a $0.5 million headwind, and the acquisition impact from RockyMounts was a $2.1 million tailwind.

Michael J Yates.

Even with inorganic help, the adventure segment was pressured by the weak demand for some legacy products and weak ticketing from lingering discounting activity. Rhino-Rack is not doing that well. MAXTRAX seems to be getting more traction and they've even managed to secure a German military contract and some new distributors in China.

Bottom Line

Macro is a concern for this business. The bottom line really is that we want to see how the shelves clear in a higher price situation. CLAR is working to defend and grow margin and profitability. While structural measures, like selling the unprofitable PIEPS business will help, we want to see how end consumers are going to respond to price increases taken up in July before we get excited about this stock at languishing prices. We do not want to take a position against a difficult economic direction and will focus on outstanding questions around the earnings direction for now, not thinking about valuations yet.
